data.title: Mercedes: China And E.U. Energy Risks Make It A Hold
data.description:
European carmakers are having a hard time this century. Mercedes (OTCPK:MBGAF) (OTCPK:MBGYY) is no exception. Its share price is barely over half of what it was at its all-time high reached almost two decades ago. What is worse, there are few positive trends that one can point to in any of its major markets, with mostly downside risk factors on the horizon.

There are also reasons to be optimistic about Mercedes' long-term potential. It has a strong brand name, and it has invested wisely in many respects.

However, I cannot feel confident in investing in its shares due to current risk factors, such as the rising probability of yet another energy crisis in Europe. There are also risks of retaliation against the company in China, as EU-China relations continue to sour. These issues need to be settled one way or another. For now, it remains a hold.

A Weak Quarterly Result And Warning Signs Of More To Come As Q3 Sales Data Come In

As the company's Q3 sales results came in, it looked like two of its major markets were struggling. Sales in China declined by 27% Y-O-Y, while sales in the US declined by 17% in the same period. This does not bode well for its next quarterly financial report, after it already experienced a weak performance in the Second Quarter of this year.

In the first half of the year, it saw a 9% decline in revenues, to 66.4 billion euros, and a 56% decline in net earnings, to 2.7 billion euros. Its profit margin is currently about 4%. Its earnings per share of 2.69 euros, or about $3, for the first half of the year, compared with its yearly dividend per share of $4.44, has me wondering whether or not the generous dividend that Mercedes currently offers has staying power, if financial results do not improve soon. Based on the latest sales data, an improvement in its financial results might not be forthcoming.

The meager returns of less than 30% in terms of share price appreciation over a period of 20 years highlight the importance of that generous dividend yield, currently at over 7%.

A Weak Quarterly Result And Warning Signs Of More To Come As Q3 Sales Data Come In

Mercedes has three main markets, namely Europe, the US, and China.

Of the three markets, Europe & China are by far the largest, but the US is also important for the company, and it is, in my view, where Mercedes still has hope of increasing its sales.

Sales In China Were Negatively Impacted By Competition

China's new vehicle market is still somewhat robust, even though it is showing signs of stalling out. Up until this decade, Mercedes car sales followed along with the trend, and it looked like China would be its most important market by far.

My estimate for the whole of 2025 is based on the latest 27% Y-O-Y decline that Mercedes just reported in the Chinese market, which is part of a broader trend that many European carmakers are experiencing in China. It seems that the era of European carmakers relying on the Chinese market to make up for weak growth in the domestic European market is coming to an end, and it has gone in reverse.

Looking beyond this year, the outlook is uncertain for Mercedes sales in China. On one hand, the trend of European carmakers being pushed out of the Chinese car market by domestic carmakers could continue at the current pace. On the other hand, China might actually want European carmakers to continue having a stake in the Chinese market, to use it as leverage if Europe tries to curtail surging EV sales in Europe that Chinese brands like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY) are experiencing. Mercedes will probably see continued downward pressure on Chinese sales, but the pace of decline will slow after this year.

The European Car Market Outlook Is Bleak For Mercedes, Based On A Weak Economy, Difficulties Related To The 2035 Combustion Engine Ban, And Chinese Carmakers Gaining Market Share

Europe is the second-largest market for Mercedes cars after China. It is officially its domestic market, but it makes up less than one-third of its total car sales. European car sales are yet to recover to 2019 levels, when just under 18 million vehicles were sold. Last year, there were just under 13 million vehicles sold on the continent. Mercedes was no exception to the trend of declining sales in Europe. It sold just over 1 million cars in total in 2019, versus 640,000 last year.

One of the main reasons why European car sales have fallen so far below levels from the last decade is that the European economy has been stagnant since 2020. A new energy crisis is potentially flying under the market's radar, with current natural gas inventories significantly below the five-year average.

So far, the markets have been remarkably calm over what seems to be a developing crisis, potentially becoming severe, depending on the weather this winter. If a shortfall occurs, it will most likely lead to yet another European economic downturn, which could have an outsized impact on an already struggling economy.

Aside from the struggling European economy, the 2035 ICE-vehicle sales ban is creating a situation where European automakers are ramping up EV sales, even as demand for them is not necessarily ever going to match that of ICE cars. In other words, as a result of forcing the European consumer market to go strictly electric in terms of personal vehicles, overall European sales are likely to decline significantly compared with sales volumes we saw before the start of this decade. I addressed this concept in an article I wrote a few years back for those who want to understand it more in-depth.

On top of the pressures that European carmakers, including Mercedes, are feeling from having to sell only EVs on the EU market by 2035, which I predict will lead to a much smaller overall car market, there is the rising tide of Chinese EV sales in Europe. When BYD starts producing its EVs in Hungary as early as the end of this year, its share of the European EV market can be expected to rise dramatically. It will also target the European market with production in Turkey and possibly Spain.

Mercedes is by no means looking to abandon its domestic market. It recently announced plans to double its production capacity at its Kecskemet factory in Hungary. The reason why this is newsworthy is that Hungary is emerging as the site where German luxury brands are pairing up with Asian EV battery makers to produce cost-competitive EVs for the European market. Hungary is set to be the world's 4th-largest EV battery producer by the end of the decade, so it makes sense for the likes of Mercedes as well as BMW (OTC:BMWKY), or Volkswagen's (OTCPK:VLKAF) Audi to center some of their EV manufacturing capacities in the country in Europe, where the batteries are also produced, making for cheaper and simpler logistics, as well as lower wages that Hungary has compared with Germany and other Western countries.

Mercedes Has Some Potential To Grow Its Presence In The Us Market, But It Is A Tough Environment For Luxury Carmakers

The EV pivot is not as prominent in the US vehicle market as it is in the European market, where it is more or less mandated by the 2035 deadline. However, in my view, EVs are eating into America's luxury car market. In other words, both the conventional luxury brand producers and EV producers are competing for the same upper-income bracket households. Tesla (TSLA) continues to lead US EV sales. The decline in Mercedes car sales in the US so far this year does not bode well for its future outlook in the US market; however, it is a market where, depending on consumer reactions to certain car concepts, anything could happen.

Investment Implications

Given the headwinds that I already identified for Mercedes, it is, in my view, not a bargain buy, even though it is trading at a share price that is roughly half compared with its all-time highs. Its forward P/E ratio of just over 10 may seem on the expensive side, but is more reasonable when compared with other carmakers. GM (GM), for instance, trades at a forward P/E ratio of just over 6, but it does not offer a notable dividend. However, GM is not faced with a possible massive hit this winter if Europe descends into another energy crisis, nor is its exposure to China as deep as Mercedes.

Mercedes is taking action to improve its competitiveness for the future, with its recent decision to expand its production capacity in Hungary being an obvious example. Average labor costs in Hungary are about 1/3 of what it costs to hire labor in Germany or many other Western European countries. It can also save on logistics by having suppliers for EV inputs, such as batteries, close by, being close to the source. However, at the moment, risk factors such as continued loss of market share in China or a potential energy crisis this winter in Europe are not factored into its current valuation; therefore, it is a hold until those factors either see some resolution or become priced in.

Risks To My Thesis

The risk of not buying Mercedes stock right now, but rather waiting for a more favorable price or for a resolution to potential downside risks, is that the company will see some signs of reversal in its fortunes in at least some or in all of its major markets, and its stock is going to rally on those signals. Given the sizable downside risks, I prefer to risk missing out, rather than risking a potentially losing investment.

A Resolution To At Least Some Of The Risks Can Signal A Time To Start Buying Mercedes Shares Incrementally

The main factor I am watching at the moment is whether or not the currently low natural gas storage levels in Europe will trigger a crisis this winter. So far, the month of October suggests that things are headed in the wrong direction, with the gap between current storage levels and the five-year average widening. Markets are yet to react to the trend, so perhaps there is something that investors are banking on to turn things around. If Europe's energy situation escalates in the next few months, there will most likely be a negative impact on the broader European markets; therefore, the risk becomes a reality that will be priced into Mercedes shares. Mercedes could then be a buying opportunity after the fact. If it turns out that Europe is safe from experiencing natural gas shortages or price spikes this winter, then the risk dissipates, and therefore it no longer needs to be factored into its share price.

Either way, it will be prudent to wait, perhaps until we get closer to the midpoint of the impending heating season, before I start buying Mercedes shares incrementally.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Is Retail Actually The Smart Money?
data.description:
I do a fair bit of institution-watching, that is, following the moves of institutional investors, asset managers, hedge funds, etc., or better known colloquially as "smart money."

Here is a quick primer from my last article on the subject:

...before we get further into this article...I encounter a slew of the same comments across all of these kinds of articles discussing institutional data.

First, the epithet of the group can be perceived as divisive, but I don't make the rules nor set monikers. The term itself is intended to articulate the sizable advantages institutional investors hold related to resources, tools, and formal expertise in the aggregate over retail investors. It is not intended to belittle retail investors' intelligence, although it is reasonable to expect that it comes across that way in many cases. If it bothers you, try thinking of it as "capital allocators" instead. Less catchy and fun to read, but just as accurate without all the bruised egos.

Second, there have been numerous studies done over the years that show the performance of institutions as it relates to how "smart money" performance is collected. There is no definitive source, as the returns of institutions vary by institution and often are measured differently by institutions than retail investors; e.g., the performance of hedge funds is often compared to the S&P 500, although they do not compare themselves that way because their goal is not to create equity-like returns...

Regardless of the pre-empting I did (there are several more paragraphs after those), the comments were still hung up on the same topic about whether "smart money" is right or correct or actually "smart" at all. I can't run away from it. Of course, not all comments were about that, but I felt like there was enough to say that the consensus of commenters (not necessarily readers, as I don't know their opinions if they didn't comment) was that smart money isn't smart at all.

The issue, as I raised in the quoted text above, is that there is no way to really quantify the performance of these capital allocators, since investors should be evaluated on how well they met their objectives (not just against the S&P 500's returns), and the objectives of institutions are difficult to measure from the outside.

So, instead of looking further into the institutional data to answer these questions, I began to look at data from retail traders. Is my audience, largely non-institutional investors, right?

Let's look at the data we have on where retail is positioned right now.

Retail Activity is at All-Time Highs

Several things came together all at once during the COVID-19 pandemic that changed retail flows, maybe forever. While the government was practicing "helicopter money" policies, they made flush with cash many people who truthfully didn't need it. For many of these folks, this was the first time they had a bunch of excess cash lying around. Why not invest it?

We created a new class of retail investor, alongside all the investors from pre-2020 that benefited from the massive rise in stock prices and valuations, who finally got the first group to listen about putting money away every month into that 401(K) and may have increased their own contributions.

The jump is easy to see when mapping retail flows into both stocks and ETFs.

This change was nowhere more pronounced than in young people; the under-40 crowd has never held a higher equity value in their portfolios, finally surpassing the 2021 peak this year.

Compare that to "smart money" allocations to the S&P 500. Looking at futures flows, which is far from the totality of holdings but does show the institutional directional bias, we can see that dealers (market makers), leveraged funds, and other traders with portfolios large enough that they are required to report ("whales") are not nearly as unified and definitely not nearly as long.

Asset managers are our closest comparison to the goals of most retail investors, so it makes sense that they are long as well, but their current net holdings sit below 2024 levels.

They Are Very Bullish...

Of the last 26 weeks, or half a year, retail traders have been net buyers in 23 of those weeks. This comes after a few small sell-offs into the market weakness we saw in February-April.

One piece of good news is that this is being done mostly with new money or stockpiled money that had been sitting in money market funds, etc. Current margin debt levels are growing, but not nearly at the same pace we see in previous bubbles.

During the dot-com bubble, investors ran up margin debts at 80% YoY, and above 70% in 2021. A figure of 35% today doesn't seem so bad by comparison.

...And They Like Options

One of the explanations I have for the reduced margin levels is the expansion of retail options activity.

Exclude all the options-related ETFs, as when they trade, they are technically counted in "smart money" stats, since institutional rules govern most funds. In fact, most of the covered call ETFs I cover either use in-house-built derivatives (can't get more institutional than that) or use off-exchange options like the very popular FLEX options offered by the OCC.

If we added the amount of retail money invested in options-based funds, it would be astronomical on top of this data from Citadel, showing its retail clients trading options in droves.

We are at multi-year highs in options volume, with the only other comparison being the meme stock bubble in January 2021, when GameStop (GME) et al. exploded.

Citadel also tracks the direction of these options. For the last 24 weeks, essentially since April, retail has been buying far more calls than puts and selling more puts than calls. The skew is wide, indicating just how bullish retail is currently.

It's not just trend chasing, necessarily, because institutional flows don't mirror this move at all. Institutional flows have taken the other side of the trade 8 out of the last 9 weeks and throughout the year. There were only a few times when their flows and retail flows aligned.

This makes sense to me, as it boils down to institutions hedging a lot more than retail does, and that shouldn't necessarily be a surprise to anyone.

Cash Is At Multi-Year Lows

Schwab (SCHW) clients have burned through their cash piles; this chart includes money markets but not cash equivalents, so we may be missing out on some investors who are holding T-Bill ETFs like SGOV over money markets, but I don't expect that blind spot to be the reason we're seeing the current trend in cash balances.

What's most interesting about this stat is that institutional traders are also historically low on cash, at least as of last month. They are sitting at levels they haven't seen since 2021 and 2013 before that.

Conclusion

Many institutional investors would likely conclude that retail investors are too bullish and are ripe to be shaken out by a prolonged crash. This is why they hedge.

Is the retail crowd the smart money now, with its near-permanent bullishness? After all, their portfolios are at record highs, they are plowing more money into the markets than ever before, and they are driving up options activity so much that they are no longer just an outlier but a player in the system to be respected.

The last two years have proven them right, and the crashes that we have seen since then have been fleeting and so not meaningful enough to be anything other than a dip to be bought by the retail masses.

I've learned that I'm less bullish than the general retail crowd; I already knew that anecdotally from comments, but the data confirms it. Perhaps I am overthinking the markets, just like the "smart money" institutions that get such a bad rap in my comments section.

It may serve me best to stop worrying and learn to love retail bullishness. Time will tell.

I'm not at that point yet and will continue to watch smart money flows.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: CME: Exchange Operator's Bullish Streak Should Continue As Market Data Segment Grows
data.description:
The Stock: A Major Exchange Operator With A Recent Earnings Beat This Week

There are always those few stocks that I keep coming back to for their interesting case. This time I am following up on major financial exchange operator CME Group Inc. (NASDAQ:CME) for my 4th edition of covering this company.

Since my very first article in May 2023, when I called it a Buy, this stock is up nearly +47%, justifying my longer-term bullish projection. Subsequently, the stock is up since my Dec. 2024 coverage, too, but slightly down since my July 2025 buy rating, showing it has pulled back slightly.

Common factors driving all of my past bullish calls include the firm's investment-grade credit rating, growing demand for the firm's data analytics solutions, and trading volumes.

For readers new to my articles, or to this company and sector, what we can see from its Seeking Alpha profile is that CME is a Chicago-based firm operating a market for trading options and futures, but also other solutions including market data services.

Since CME just released their latest quarterly earnings on Oct. 22nd, I've updated my prior research. Based on updated data, my thesis today argues that this stock should actually be upgraded to a Strong Buy this time.

Some key upside factors included in my rating worksheet below include several growth drivers, impressive margins, an investment-grade rating from Fitch and low D/E among peers, proven long-term dividend growth, and despite elevated fwd P/E valuation there is an additional +5% upside expected in the share price.

Rating Score: Strong Buy

Growth Drivers: Demand For Market Data and 24/7 Trading Key To Focus On

In this section, I'll touch on some points related to growth drivers, both macro-level and company-specific.

Global expansion is a growth driver, I think, and on that note I would mention that CME announced this month "its expansion into the Middle East with the opening of its Dubai office."

As a longtime analyst of business information systems, what particularly interested me about CME is one notable point from the recent quarterly results is that there seems to be increased top-line growth momentum in the firm's market data/information services practice:

So, that is a key area of growth I think investors should pay attention to. In fact, the quarterly presentation (pg. 6) highlighted that the market data practice was "driven by a growing subscriber count and the price increase that took effect in January."

What I like typically about a subscription-based model is that subscriptions signed this year can drive future revenues, assuming those clients stay on board.

Further, in terms of overall top-line growth, besides YoY revenue growth I would point out there is expected positive fwd revenue growth of +6.7%, very close to the sector average:

Additionally, from a "future" growth perspective, I think an upside driver could come from 24/7 trading, and recently the Investing.com news desk wrote:

The company is preparing for 24/7 cryptocurrency trading in 2026 and exploring tokenization opportunities with Google Cloud. Strategic initiatives also include expanding event-based contracts and focusing on retail strategy and market innovation.

From a macro view, there are some additional signs of growing demand for cryptocurrency trading, which of course needs a marketplace to function efficiently. For instance, Investopedia reported last spring that Israel-based trading platform eToro is filing for a U.S. IPO, as the company said a major part of their recent success is "an increase in crypto assets trading, which made up 38% of eToro’s commissions in 2024, more than doubling from 17% in 2023."

So, the above points are multiple growth factors without any significant offsetting downside factors, so for this reason I am going with a Strong Buy in this category.

Profitability: A Case of High Margins And Low Expense Growth

Next, let's talk about the topic of profitability.

Margins are key to this story, as I think they give a sense of a company's profit efficiency, in relation to peers.

Notable in that regard are some impressive headline margin numbers, such as CME's EBITDA margin of nearly +71% (vs sector avg of 21.5%) and a net income margin of nearly +59% (vs sector avg of 23%).

In my research, I did a comparison against 3 peers in the segment of financial exchanges and data, and these are NYSE parent company Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), Moody's (MCO), and Nasdaq (NDAQ):

From this comparison, data shows that CME easily beats these 3 peers on both profit margins. In case you're curious why I consider both of these margins, it's because the EBITDA excludes non-cash expenses like depreciation, so to me it is even more indicative of the core business profit efficiency.

Although these margins are trailing and not forward-looking, since they are based on data until now, I think it shows this firm has already proven itself with a model of profit efficiency, and this should boost its case as a serious company. Future earnings, of course, will depend on these margins maintained or improving even more.

In addition, CME's quarterly results (pg 6) showed some interesting data, in that total expenses have risen less than 1% on a YoY basis, which indicates the firm is keeping costs under control, which I think helps margins:

Lastly, on the topic of earnings, we cannot ignore what the consensus says as I think it could influence investor behavior, and in this case the consensus expects between 4-8% YoY EPS growth in each of the coming fiscal years, so this seems like a modestly positive forward view.

So, given that positive EPS view, the low expense growth, and the strong margins among peers, I am highly bullish in this category as well.

Balance Sheet & Credit Rating: Investment-Grade, and Very Low D/E vs Peers

In this section, I want to discuss some factors impacting balance sheet risk.

I think recently positive credit ratings from major agencies like Fitch and others adds a strong angle to a firm's risk profile, that investors should consider. For instance, back in February Fitch affirmed an investment-grade "AA-" rating, saying in its rating note that there are some positive future-looking factors and "views CME's predominantly long-term funding profile and demonstrated ability to raise long-duration debt favorably."

Next, I think it is worth comparing not just debt levels but debt in relation to equity, and comparing the debt/equity ratio vs. peers, here are the results:

In this data, we see CME has a D/E of only 0.13, which is extremely low and the lowest in this peer group, but also consider the trend line over 3 years shows that this D/E has not risen significantly in that time, indicating lower leverage risk that some peers.

Given the strong credit rating, and low D/E, and also lack of exposure to bad loans or commercial real estate (such is the case with many banks) this financial firm presents an interesting case of attractive balance sheet risk, and for this reason gets a buy rating from me in this category, however I would not overcomplicate this discussion today since the balance sheet is only one component of the overall rating.

Cashflow & Dividend Trends: Proven Growth In Both

Moving along, I am combining the discussion of dividend and cashflow trends.

As for dividends, for yield-driven investors I would point out the fwd dividend yield of +4% (vs. sector avg of 3%), but for growth-driven dividend investors this company can boast a proven track record of +9.5% dividend growth CAGR over 10 years, visualized as follows:

I think this shows evidence that CME has made a longer-term commitment to return capital to shareholders, even in the wake of the 2020 global pandemic when it did not seem to significantly cut its dividend, a sign it could be better equipped to withstand such economic shocks in the future too, than some firms and sectors that I've seen (such as some hotels and airlines) which had to cut dividends in the wake of the pandemic. As an example, Delta Airlines (DAL) had no dividends in 2021 and 2022.

Cashflow trends need to support the dividend, however, and what I want to point out in that regard when it comes to CME is its core operating cashflow growth was +17.5% (vs sector avg of 11.3%), while expected forward growth is +8.55% (slightly behind the sector avg). Both of these are positive factors to consider in the cashflow picture, since operating cashflow reflects the core business and not financing or investing.

Again, these points above only add to the already bullish sentiment in this article up until now.

Valuation & Share Price: A 5% Further Price Appreciation Expected, In Wake of Bullish Stock Direction

Coming towards the closing topic in this article, let's get into the realm of valuation and share price.

A forward-looking valuation metric worth considering is the fwd P/E, in this case both the non-GAAP and GAAP-based multiple is around 26x, so let's use that.

Both are significantly overvalued vs the sector average, and in addition we can see from momentum data that CME has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) in the last 6 months, in terms of price return.

Additionally, for a considerable time, this stock has been trading above its 200-day SMA ever since last year:

So, this is a good example where the stock has elevated valuation, momentum vs the S&P 500, and is trading above average, so it seems they all align this time and indicate strong market confidence about the future potential of this company.

Considering the evidence I presented including strong profit margins, a positive future EPS growth consensus, and low leverage risk, I think this market bullishness is more than justified. However, in order to buy or hold this stock at this time, there should also be adequate further upside in the share price, so on that topic I made this price target worksheet:

In the worksheet above, the data shows I have a price target on this stock of $279.64, while the avg Wall Street consensus target is $282.59, and on average both expect around a +5% upside vs the last closing price.

With that said, I think a 5% upside is relatively low for a new buyer, so in this category it moves the needle more into the hold column, however keep in mind this is only one factor in my overall rating and not decisive by itself.

Conclusion: More Bullish Than Consensus

In conclusion, my rating today of a Strong Buy is even more bullish than the consensus from analysts, Wall Street, and the quant system.

Despite a low price upside forecast, the other factors discussed today more than offset that factor and drive this stock into the strong buy category, including income statement, dividend, cashflow, and balance sheet factors.

However, it is fair to also consider a risk that could challenge my confident outlook, and one that has not yet been included in this article is that a core business of this firm's relies on trading volumes in the options and futures markets, since CME makes money on that volume, to put it simply. This volume can continually fluctuate, and we can visualize that in the chart below from CME:

Because of such volatility, I think investors should expect fluctuation in future revenue in that part of the business, but a mitigating factor overall is that CME has other business segments too, such as the market data segment and subscription-driven businesses, thereby diversifying the future revenue picture.

Therefore, my call-to-action is to continue betting on exchanges like this with a combination strong fundamentals and continued market momentum.
